passageSets.title: Two Pictures of Democracy
passageSets.passage:
When citizens are asked what democracy means, most reach instinctively for the image of a vote being counted. On this familiar picture, a democracy is a vast machine for tallying preferences: each person registers a want, the wants are summed, and the majority prevails. The machine is admirably neutral, for it asks no one to justify a preference and privileges no one's reasons over another's. Yet a rival picture has long competed for our allegiance, one that treats the silent ballot as democracy's thinnest moment rather than its essence. On this second view, the heart of self-government lies not in counting what citizens already want but in the public conversation through which they decide what is worth wanting. The contrast between these two ideals, aggregative and deliberative, structures nearly every serious quarrel about democratic reform.

The aggregative ideal has obvious virtues, and its defenders are right to insist on them. Counting votes treats citizens as equals in the most literal way, since the banker and the bus driver each contribute exactly one unit to the total. It demands no eloquence, no leisure for study, and no taste for argument, qualities that are unequally distributed and easily faked. Above all, it is honest about disagreement: when people want incompatible things, aggregation does not pretend that more talk will dissolve the conflict, but simply registers the numbers and moves on. Critics, however, notice what the tally ignores. A preference formed in ignorance counts as much as one formed in wisdom, and a want manufactured by advertisers counts as much as a considered conviction. The machine records the surface of opinion while remaining indifferent to its sources.

The deliberative ideal begins precisely from this complaint. Its proponents argue that legitimate decisions must emerge from a process in which citizens exchange reasons that others could in principle accept, rather than merely brandishing the preferences they happened to arrive with. On this view, the test of a policy is not how many people favor it but whether it could survive open scrutiny in a public forum where every affected party may speak. Deliberation is said to refine raw desire into something more defensible, exposing selfish demands to the discipline of having to be stated as general principles. It promises, too, a kind of education: in justifying ourselves to strangers we are forced to imagine their circumstances, and the habit of justification slowly enlarges our sense of the common good. Where aggregation counts heads, deliberation hopes to change minds.

And yet the deliberative picture carries a flattering illusion that its admirers too rarely confront. To prize reasoned argument is, however unintentionally, to reward those already fluent in its conventions, the educated and the articulate, while the inarticulate citizen, whose grievance is real but whose phrasing is clumsy, finds the forum no friendlier than the marketplace. A conversation can exclude as efficiently as a poll tax when the price of admission is a confident command of public speech. The honest conclusion, then, is not that one ideal should vanquish the other but that each corrects the other's characteristic blindness. Aggregation guards against the quiet tyranny of the eloquent; deliberation guards against the thoughtless tyranny of the counted. A democracy worthy of the name must somehow hold both pictures in view, suspicious of any reformer who offers only one.

questions.stem: The author's primary purpose in the passage is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: argue that the aggregative and deliberative ideals each compensate for a characteristic weakness in the other

choices.label: B
choices.text: demonstrate that the deliberative ideal is superior to the aggregative ideal in every respect

choices.label: C
choices.text: trace the historical development of voting procedures in modern democracies

choices.label: D
choices.text: defend the aggregative ideal against the objections raised by its critics

questions.explanation: A is correct because the passage sets up two ideals and concludes that each corrects the other's blindness, insisting a democracy must hold both in view. B is wrong because the author criticizes deliberation's flattering illusion rather than declaring it superior in every respect. C is wrong because no history of voting procedures is traced; the treatment is conceptual throughout. D is wrong because, although aggregation's virtues are noted, the author also exposes its limits and never makes its defense the thesis.

choices.label: B

choices.label: C

choices.label: D
choices.text: The remark that deliberation 'hopes to change minds.'

hints.rationale: The final paragraph's both-in-view conclusion states the author's overall aim, so it points you to the purpose.

hints.prompt: One tempting answer says the author's purpose is to show the deliberative ideal is superior in every respect. Why is that a trap?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The author criticizes deliberation's 'flattering illusion' and never crowns it superior, so that overstates his position.

choices.label: B

choices.label: C

choices.label: D

hints.rationale: Because the author faults deliberation as well as aggregation, 'superior in every respect' misreads a deliberately balanced thesis.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the deliberative ideal is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: unqualified enthusiasm for its capacity to educate citizens

choices.label: B
choices.text: sympathy tempered by awareness of its tendency to exclude the inarticulate

choices.label: C
choices.text: open contempt for an impractical and elitist fantasy

choices.label: D
choices.text: detached neutrality that refuses to evaluate it at all

questions.explanation: B is correct because the author credits deliberation's promise to refine desire and educate citizens yet warns of its flattering illusion and its exclusion of the inarticulate, which is sympathy tempered by reservation. A is wrong because the enthusiasm is explicitly qualified, not unqualified. C is wrong because contempt is too strong; the author grants the ideal genuine value. D is wrong because the author plainly evaluates the ideal rather than remaining neutral.

questions.stem: The reference to a 'poll tax' in the final paragraph functions primarily to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: propose a concrete reform that would make deliberation more inclusive

choices.label: B
choices.text: remind readers of a specific historical injustice in voting law for its own sake

choices.label: C
choices.text: underscore that deliberation can bar participation as effectively as an explicit barrier

choices.label: D
choices.text: concede that aggregation, too, has historically excluded the poor

questions.explanation: C is correct because the poll tax image is an analogy showing that requiring a command of public speech can shut people out of the forum as effectively as a fee shuts them out of the ballot. A is wrong because no concrete reform is proposed; the reference is critical, not constructive. B is wrong because the phrase is used for comparison, not for the sake of its own history. D is wrong because the analogy targets deliberation's exclusions, not aggregation's.

questions.stem: Which scenario would the author most likely cite as illustrating the central weakness of the deliberative ideal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A national referendum in which a narrow majority overrules a better-informed minority

choices.label: B
choices.text: A legislature that adopts a policy after no public debate whatsoever

choices.label: C
choices.text: A town that decides its budget by drawing proposals at random

choices.label: D
choices.text: A community forum in which a soft-spoken resident's valid concern is dismissed because she argues less smoothly than her polished neighbors

questions.explanation: D is correct because it dramatizes deliberation rewarding the fluent and silencing a citizen with a real but clumsily argued concern, exactly the weakness the author identifies. A is wrong because overruling an informed minority illustrates a defect of aggregation, not deliberation. B is wrong because the absence of debate is a failure to deliberate at all, not a flaw internal to deliberation. C is wrong because random selection is neither aggregative nor deliberative and is not the author's concern.

choices.label: A

choices.label: B

choices.label: D

hints.prompt: Two scenarios are tempting: 'a majority overrules a better-informed minority' and 'a legislature adopts a policy after no debate.' Why can both be set aside?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The first illustrates a flaw of aggregation, and the second is a failure to deliberate at all, so neither is a flaw internal to deliberation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deliberation has no weaknesses at all, so both must be wrong.

choices.label: C

choices.label: D

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most weaken the author's claim that deliberation tends to favor the already-articulate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Educated participants speak more often than others in most recorded public forums

choices.label: B
choices.text: Structured forums that give each participant equal speaking time and coaching produce decisions in which less-educated citizens prevail as often as the educated

choices.label: C
choices.text: Citizens report enjoying deliberative forums more than casting ballots

choices.label: D
choices.text: Most policies endorsed in deliberative forums are later approved by majority vote

questions.explanation: B is correct because if simple design changes such as equal speaking time neutralize the eloquence advantage, the bias is not intrinsic to deliberation, which weakens the author's claim. A is wrong because it supports, rather than weakens, the claim that the educated dominate. C is wrong because enjoyment of forums says nothing about who actually prevails in them. D is wrong because later majority approval does not reveal whether the articulate dominated the deliberation itself.

questions.stem: The author's argument depends on which of the following assumptions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Citizens never change their preferences once they have formed them

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eloquence and wisdom are always found in the same individuals

choices.label: C
choices.text: The ability to argue persuasively in public is distributed unequally among citizens

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aggregative and deliberative procedures cannot be combined within one system

questions.explanation: C is correct because the charge that deliberation rewards the fluent presupposes that such fluency is distributed unequally; otherwise no one would be disadvantaged by it. A is wrong because the deliberative ideal the author describes assumes preferences can change, the opposite of this. B is wrong because the author implies eloquence and wisdom can diverge, as in the clumsy but genuine grievance. D is wrong because the conclusion assumes the two ideals can be combined, so their incompatibility is not an assumption the argument requires.

choices.label: A

hints.prompt: A tempting answer is that the argument assumes 'the two ideals cannot be combined within one system.' Why can it be eliminated?

choices.label: A

passageSets.title: The Manufacture of Nations
passageSets.passage:
Nothing seems more natural than the nation. We speak of the French character or the German soul as though peoples were features of the landscape, as old as their rivers and as fixed as their mountains. National holidays summon ancestors, anthems stir something that feels deeper than mere opinion, and citizens who would hesitate to die for a tax code will die without hesitation for a flag. Yet the historian who looks closely finds that most nations are startlingly young. The very borders, languages, and traditions that patriots treat as immemorial were, more often than not, assembled within the last two centuries by states that needed loyal populations. The nation, in short, presents itself as something discovered when it is largely something made, and the work of making it has been disguised as the work of remembering.

Consider how the manufacture proceeds. A state draws a boundary on a map, and the people inside it, who may speak a dozen dialects and worship in rival ways, are informed that they constitute a single folk. Schools then teach a common language, often suppressing the others, and a shared history is written in which scattered episodes are arranged into one heroic story marching toward the present. Printers and, later, broadcasters allow a peasant in one province to read the same morning news as a clerk a thousand miles away, so that strangers who will never meet come to picture themselves as members of one body. None of this requires conspiracy; it requires only schoolrooms, censuses, and the daily repetition of a name. Through such ordinary machinery a population is slowly persuaded that it was always a nation merely waiting to awaken.

To call the nation constructed is not, however, to call it a fraud, and here many debunkers overreach. A cathedral is also constructed, stone by deliberate stone, yet no one concludes that it offers no real shelter. An identity assembled by schools and newspapers can become, within a generation, as genuine a part of a person as a language learned in childhood; it shapes what one loves, mourns, and is willing to sacrifice. The sentiment is manufactured and sincere at once, and the cynic who sees only the manufacturing understands the nation no better than the patriot who sees only the sincerity. What the constructed character of nationhood does establish is something more modest and more unsettling: that arrangements which feel inevitable were in fact chosen, and that what was chosen could have been, and still might be, chosen otherwise.

This last point is where the argument earns its edge. If nations are made, then they can be remade, and the same machinery that once stitched strangers into a people can be turned to crueler patterns. The schoolbook that teaches a unifying story can be rewritten to teach who does not belong, and the press that lets distant citizens imagine a shared body can teach them to imagine a shared enemy. The danger of nationalism lies not in fellow feeling as such, which is among the more generous of human capacities, but in the forgetting on which it depends, the conviction that an invented boundary is a fact of nature and therefore beyond question. A clear-eyed citizen need not despise the nation. She need only remember that it was built, and ask, each time she is told it could not be otherwise, who profits from her belief that it could not.

questions.stem: The central thesis of the passage is that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: nationalism is a harmful illusion that clear-eyed citizens should reject entirely

choices.label: B
choices.text: nations are ancient communities whose traditions states have faithfully preserved

choices.label: C
choices.text: the emotional power of national feeling proves that nations are natural rather than made

choices.label: D
choices.text: nations are modern constructions whose manufactured origins are concealed, though this does not make national feeling unreal

questions.explanation: D is correct because the passage argues that nations are made by states yet insists the resulting sentiment is manufactured and sincere at once. A is wrong because the author explicitly says a citizen need not despise the nation, ruling out total rejection. B is wrong because it inverts the thesis; traditions were assembled, not faithfully preserved. C is wrong because the author denies that emotional power proves naturalness, noting that constructed things can still move us.

questions.stem: The author's overall attitude toward nationalism can best be characterized as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: wary appreciation, recognizing its genuine power while warning against its capacity for harm

choices.label: B
choices.text: nostalgic admiration for the unifying traditions it preserves

choices.label: D
choices.text: alarmed hostility that regards all national feeling as dangerous

D is wrong because the author says fellow feeling is generous and that the citizen need not despise the nation, so the hostility is not wholesale.

C is wrong because the analogy is not about worship or about criticizing patriots. D is wrong because the comparison concerns reality despite construction, not eventual decay.

passageSets.title: What Money Cannot Buy
passageSets.passage:
A generation of economists has taught us to see the market as a marvel of coordination, and on its own terms the lesson is sound. Prices gather scattered knowledge no planner could assemble, steering scarce goods toward those who value them most without anyone needing to give an order. The trouble begins when this genuine insight hardens into a creed, the conviction that whatever can be bought and sold should be, and that any objection to a transaction is mere squeamishness dressed up as principle. Against this creed it is worth recalling a stubborn fact that markets themselves cannot price: some goods are altered, and sometimes ruined, by being treated as commodities. To ask what a thing costs is occasionally to misunderstand what it is, and a society that forgets this distinction does not become merely richer or poorer but coarser in a way its ledgers cannot record.

Consider the friend who can be hired. A companion paid by the hour may perform every outward office of friendship, listening to troubles and offering counsel, yet the moment money changes hands the relation becomes something else, a service rather than a bond, precisely because friendship is partly constituted by its not being for sale. The same corrosion attacks honors that are purchased rather than earned, apologies extracted by contract rather than offered in remorse, and votes bought rather than freely cast. In each case the good depends on a motive that payment drives out. A prize loses its meaning when the prize is the money; an apology bought is no apology at all. The market does not merely allocate these goods badly; by allocating them at all it dissolves the very thing it claims to distribute.

The economist has a ready reply: if two adults consent to a trade and no third party is harmed, who are we to forbid it? This appeal to consent is powerful, and it rightly warns us against the smugness of those who would police other people's bargains. But it quietly assumes the very thing in dispute, namely that the good in question survives being sold unchanged, so that the only questions left are about willingness and harm. When the act of pricing transforms the good, consent cannot rescue it, for the parties are no longer trading the thing they imagine. A man who sells his vote consents freely and harms no stranger, yet he has still helped convert a public trust into private property, degrading a practice on which others silently rely. Some losses fall on no identifiable victim and are real all the same.

None of this licenses a blanket suspicion of buying and selling, and the argument would collapse into sentimentality if it did. Most goods are simply goods, indifferent to the manner of their exchange; bread nourishes whether given or sold, and there is no nobler loaf in the gift. The claim is narrower and therefore harder to dismiss: a particular class of goods carries meanings that money corrodes, and deciding which goods belong to that class is a moral question that economics is not equipped to answer. The market is an excellent servant and a disastrous master, and the task of a thoughtful public is to keep drawing, and redrawing, the line between what may be priced and what must not. To pretend the line does not exist is not hardheaded realism. It is a moral judgment disguised as the refusal to make one.

passageSets.title: The Rational Actor on Trial
passageSets.passage:
At the center of orthodox economics sits a single, audacious figure: the rational actor, a person who knows what she wants, ranks her options consistently, and chooses whatever best serves her ends. This figure has been mocked for two centuries as a bloodless caricature, and the mockery is partly deserved. No one actually computes the way the model pretends. Yet the assumption has earned its keep, for it lets economists derive sharp predictions from spare premises, and those predictions are often startlingly accurate in the aggregate. When the price of a good rises, people buy less of it, just as the model says, whatever the tangle of motives in any single shopper's head. The defenders of rationality have therefore long argued that realism of assumptions is beside the point; a model should be judged not by whether its premises are literally true but by whether its forecasts hold.

The behavioral critics have nonetheless landed real blows, and their evidence is harder to wave away than the old armchair objections. People do not merely deviate from the rational ideal at random, in errors that cancel out; they deviate in patterned, predictable directions. They fear a loss more than they relish an equivalent gain, so they cling to failing investments long past reason. They judge probabilities by how easily an example springs to mind, overrating dramatic risks and shrugging at common ones. They are swayed by how a choice is framed, accepting a procedure described as saving lives that they reject when it is described, identically, in terms of lives lost. If the mistakes were scattered noise, the rational model would still work on average, as its defenders claim. But systematic bias does not average away, and a model blind to it will mispredict in exactly the cases where the stakes, and the manipulations, are highest.

Still, the critics can overreach as badly as the dogmatists they correct. To show that people are sometimes predictably foolish is not to show that they are always so, nor that the rational model is useless. In thick markets, where mistakes are punished and the careless are gradually replaced by the shrewd, behavior often converges on what the model predicts even when no individual reasons as it assumes. A catalogue of biases is not yet a theory, and a theory that abandons the elegant rational core for a sprawling list of exceptions may forecast worse, not better, by drowning signal in detail. The honest position concedes what each side sees. Human beings are neither the flawless calculators of the textbook nor the helpless dupes of the laboratory, and any model that insists on one portrait will stumble wherever the other is true.

The deepest mistake, then, is to ask whether people are rational, as though the answer were a fact awaiting discovery. Rationality is not a property of human beings to be confirmed or refuted but an assumption to be adopted or dropped depending on the question at hand. For forecasting how a market absorbs a tax, the spare model may serve admirably. For designing a retirement plan that real, distractible workers will actually use, it is worse than useless, and the framing effects the critics document become the very levers of good policy. Maturity in economics lies in knowing which tool the moment demands, holding the rational actor lightly, as a convenience rather than a faith. The figure at the center of the discipline is best treated the way a mapmaker treats a straight line through rough country, indispensable and never to be mistaken for the terrain.

passageSets.title: Breaking the Law to Honor It
passageSets.passage:
There is a familiar objection to civil disobedience that sounds like common sense and is in fact a confusion. If everyone broke the laws he personally disliked, the objection runs, society would dissolve into chaos, so the citizen who breaks even an unjust law has placed himself outside the rule of law altogether. The argument has force against the ordinary criminal, who breaks the law in secret, for private gain, and hopes to escape its penalty. But it misdescribes the conscientious lawbreaker almost entirely. The civil dissident acts openly, not covertly; he seeks no personal advantage; and far from fleeing punishment, he typically courts it, submitting to arrest as the price of his protest. These differences are not incidental. They mark civil disobedience as something closer to an appeal than an assault, a way of contesting a law from within the framework of legality rather than abandoning that framework.

The willingness to accept punishment is the hinge of the whole practice, and it does double work. First, it testifies to sincerity: a person who will go to prison for a cause has given a costly signal that he acts on conviction rather than convenience, and bystanders who might dismiss a cheap protest are forced to take a costly one seriously. Second, and more subtly, the acceptance of penalty expresses a continuing respect for law as such even while a particular law is defied. The dissident says, in effect, that he honors the legal order enough to bear its sanctions, and that his quarrel is with one unjust statute, not with the idea of being governed by rules. This is what distinguishes him from the revolutionary, who rejects the legitimacy of the whole system and would feel no obligation to submit to its courts. Civil disobedience is disobedience kept deliberately within limits.

To say this is not to bless every act that calls itself disobedience. The practice carries its own internal discipline, and the dissident who ignores it forfeits the moral standing he claims. The law defied should bear on a genuine and grave injustice, not a mere inconvenience or private grievance; ordinary political channels should have been tried and found closed or hopeless; and the protest should avoid violence, which silences the appeal it is meant to make and invites the very chaos the critics fear. These constraints are demanding, and they explain why principled disobedience is rare and revolution rarer still. They also explain why the disobedient cannot consistently complain when the state, having let him make his appeal, nonetheless enforces the penalty. He invited the punishment as part of his argument; to resent it afterward would be to misunderstand the role he chose to play.

Understood in this disciplined way, civil disobedience is not a threat to the rule of law but one of its quiet guardians. A legal order that admits no avenue for conscientious protest leaves the citizen with only two options when the law turns unjust, silent complicity or open revolt, and a wise polity prefers the middle path that disobedience provides. The dissident keeps faith with the deeper purposes of law even as he breaks one of its commands, reminding the majority that legality and justice are not the same thing and that the gap between them is the community's responsibility to close. The privilege can of course be abused by the merely stubborn. But a society confident in its own justice should welcome the challenge, having nothing to fear from an appeal it can answer.

passageSets.title: The Scarcest Resource
passageSets.passage:
The classic defense of a free press rests on a hopeful picture of the citizen. In this picture, newspapers supply the facts, citizens weigh them in calm deliberation, and public opinion forms as the considered verdict of an informed people. Censorship is the great enemy, for it starves the citizen of the facts he needs, and the remedy is more speech, more outlets, more information flowing freely into the public square. Whatever its merits in an age of scarce print, this picture has grown dangerously obsolete. The modern citizen does not suffer from too little information; he drowns in it. The binding constraint is no longer the supply of facts but the supply of attention, and once attention rather than information becomes the scarce resource, the entire logic of the free press is quietly turned on its head.

The reversal works through competition, which the older theory assumed could only help. When a hundred outlets chase the same distracted audience, each must fight to be noticed, and the surest way to be noticed is not to be the most accurate but to be the most arousing. Outrage travels faster than nuance; a vivid anecdote outperforms a careful statistic; a story that flatters what readers already believe is shared more eagerly than one that complicates it. None of this requires any editor to be cynical. A newsroom may be staffed entirely by honest people and still drift toward sensation, because the outlets that resist the drift are simply read less and eventually vanish, while those that surrender prosper and multiply. The market does not reward the press that informs the public best; it rewards the press that captures attention best, and these are not the same press, however much we wish they were.

The consequences for public life are subtle precisely because they wear no jackboot. A censored citizen knows he is being kept from something and may resent it; the citizen of the attention economy feels perfectly free, even overwhelmed by choice, and that is exactly the trap. He is not denied the important story; it is simply there, somewhere, buried beneath a thousand louder ones, and he lacks the time and the cues to dig it out. Offered endless entertainment dressed as news, he gradually loses the taste for the slower, duller business of actually governing himself. The public sphere does not collapse in a single act of suppression. It erodes grain by grain, as the habits of sustained attention that self-government requires are outcompeted by the habits that hold an audience, until citizens retain the right to know everything and the capacity to absorb almost nothing.

It would be a grave error to read this diagnosis as an argument for control. The cure for a sickness of attention cannot be a return to scarcity imposed from above, which would hand to officials the very power the free press exists to check. The point is rather that the old faith in more, the conviction that any problem of public discourse can be solved by adding outlets and removing restraints, mistakes the disease for the remedy. If the scarce resource is attention, then the institutions worth defending are those that cultivate it: schools that teach patience with difficulty, outlets insulated enough from the scramble for clicks to pursue the unexciting truth, and citizens who treat their own attention as something not to be surrendered cheaply. A free people can be rendered ignorant without a single book being banned, simply by being perpetually, profitably amused.

passageSets.title: The Forgotten Conditional
passageSets.passage:
Economists have a favorite proof that free trade benefits everyone, and like many favorite proofs it is true in a way that conceals what matters most. Open a closed economy to foreign goods, the argument runs, and the nation as a whole grows richer: consumers gain access to cheaper products, efficient industries expand, and the total pie enlarges. It is granted that some workers lose their jobs when factories move abroad, but the gains to the winners are said to exceed the losses to the losers by enough that the winners could, in principle, compensate the losers and still come out ahead. From this the conclusion is drawn that trade is good for the country, and that those who resist it are either confused about economics or selfishly defending a privilege at everyone else's expense. The proof is elegant, and as a guide to policy it has proven repeatedly, disastrously naive.

The whole defense turns on a small phrase that is easy to miss: in principle. The winners could compensate the losers; whether they actually do is a separate question, and the historical answer is mostly that they do not. The gains from trade are real but diffuse, spread thinly across millions of consumers who each save a little on cheaper goods and scarcely notice the source of their good fortune. The losses, by contrast, are concentrated and brutal, falling on particular workers in particular towns whose factories close and whose skills, built over a lifetime, suddenly command no price. A diffuse benefit generates no grateful constituency, while a concentrated loss generates a furious one, and the abstract bargain by which the lucky were supposed to repay the unlucky dissolves the moment it must pass through an actual legislature. The compensation is promised in the theorem and forgotten in the world.

Worse, the orthodox account misreads even the nature of the loss it dismisses. To the economist the displaced worker has lost a wage, a quantity that might be restored by a check, and so the failure looks like a mere accounting oversight. But a job is rarely just a wage. It is a place in a community, a source of standing, a daily proof that one is needed, and these are not things a transfer payment can replace. A town that has lost its mill has lost not only its incomes but its reason to exist as a town, and its people experience the change not as a line on a national ledger turning upward but as a verdict that the wider society no longer has any use for them. From such verdicts grow the resentments that later astonish the comfortable, who ask why people vote against their own economic interest, never grasping that economic interest was never the whole of what was taken.

To insist on all this is not to side with the protectionist, whose walls would only make the whole society poorer while protecting a few at the expense of the many. The point is narrower and more demanding. If trade truly enriches the nation as a whole, then the nation as a whole owes something real, and not merely hypothetical, to those it has asked to bear the cost of the common gain. A society that pockets the benefits of openness while leaving the displaced to absorb the losses alone has not refuted its critics; it has proved them right. The choice was never between open borders and closed ones, but between a globalization that honors its debts and one that simply pretends, with an elegant proof, that no debt was ever incurred.

passageSets.title: The Double Edge of Divided Power
passageSets.passage:
Few constitutional arrangements are praised as warmly, or as carelessly, as federalism. To divide power between a central government and the regions, its admirers say, is to build liberty into the very architecture of the state. A citizen oppressed by one level of government may appeal to another; an ambitious tyrant who would need to capture a single capital must instead subdue a dozen; and the regions become, in a celebrated phrase, laboratories in which bold policies can be tested on a small scale before a whole nation is committed to them. There is real wisdom in this praise, and the historical record offers it considerable support. Yet the praise is usually offered as though the division of power pointed in only one direction, toward freedom, when in truth the same arrangement that can shelter the citizen from a distant tyrant can also shelter a local tyrant from the citizen.

History supplies the uncomfortable examples readily, though admirers of federalism prefer to look away from them. The same regional autonomy that lets an enlightened province pioneer a humane reform lets a benighted one entrench a cruelty its national government would never have permitted. When a local majority wishes to oppress a local minority, federalism does not stand in its way; on the contrary, it hands that majority a shield, allowing it to brand outside interference as an assault on regional self-rule. The appeal to a higher level of government, which the optimistic account treats as an ever-available remedy, works only when the higher level is more just than the lower, and there is no law of nature guaranteeing that it will be. Sometimes the capital is the friend of freedom and the province its enemy; sometimes the reverse; and federalism as such takes no side in the quarrel.

Even the cherished image of the laboratory deserves a harder look. Experiments are valuable only if their results are observed, judged, and allowed to spread or die on their merits, and a federal system does not automatically supply these conditions. A region may persist for generations in a policy that has plainly failed elsewhere, insulated by pride and local power from the verdict of the evidence. Worse, the subjects of these experiments are not laboratory animals but human beings, and an experiment that ruins the lives of those trapped within its borders is not redeemed by the lesson it offers observers safely outside them. Nor do regional choices stay neatly within regional lines: pollution drifts across borders, the poorly governed export their refugees, and a race to cut taxes or wages in one region can drag down standards in all. The laboratory, in short, is rarely as isolated, as humane, or as self-correcting as the metaphor suggests.

The sensible conclusion is neither to worship federalism nor to abolish it, but to demote it from a principle to an instrument. Dividing power is neither friend nor enemy of liberty in the abstract; it is a mechanism whose effects depend entirely on who holds the divided power and how they use it. Where local majorities are decent and the center is the greater danger, federalism protects freedom; where local majorities are cruel and the center is its last hope, the very same federalism protects oppression. To argue about federalism as though it were good or bad in itself is therefore to argue about the wrong thing. The real question is never simply how power should be divided, but always, and far more awkwardly, what the holders of each portion are likely to do with it.

passageSets.title: The Myth of Desert
passageSets.passage:
The modern faith in meritocracy is, on its surface, an attractive and even generous creed. It holds that rewards should track not birth or favor but talent and effort, that the son of a laborer and the daughter of a duke should rise or fall by what they make of themselves, and that a fair society is one in which careers are open to talents. So stated, meritocracy is a weapon against privilege, and as such it has done genuine good. But the doctrine rarely stops there. It slides, almost imperceptibly, from the modest claim that the talented should be allowed to succeed to the immodest claim that the successful therefore deserve all they receive, and that the unsuccessful, having had their fair chance, have only themselves to blame. It is this second claim, the moral theory of desert lurking inside the meritocratic ideal, that cannot survive examination.

Begin with talent. No one chooses the gifts with which she is born, nor the family that nurtures them, nor the era that happens to prize them. A person who can run fast, calculate quickly, or charm a room did nothing to earn the aptitude itself; she found it in herself the way she found the color of her eyes. To be sure, raw talent must be developed, and development takes work. But here the meritocrat retreats to effort as the true ground of desert, and effort proves no firmer footing. The capacity for sustained effort, the discipline to practice when others rest, is itself partly a gift of temperament and upbringing, encouraged in some children and crushed in others before they are old enough to take credit for either. If I deserve no praise for a talent I did not choose, I can hardly deserve full praise for diligence that was, in equal measure, handed to me by a fortunate nature and a supportive home.

Suppose, however, we waved these doubts aside and granted that the diligent somehow deserve credit for their diligence. A further problem remains, and it is fatal. The market does not reward merit; it rewards scarcity and demand, which are different things entirely. A gifted teacher and a mediocre financier may work with equal devotion, yet the second is paid many times the first, not because he is more deserving but because the accident of supply and demand happens to prize his skill in this decade. Change the surrounding economy and the rewards reshuffle overnight, though no one has grown more or less deserving. What a talent earns is set by what others will pay for it, and what others will pay is a brute social fact, not a moral judgment. To treat one's paycheck as the measure of one's merit is to confuse a price with a verdict on the soul.

None of this is an argument against paying people, or even against paying them unequally. Incentives are useful, and a society may sensibly reward scarce and valuable work in order to call it forth, without pretending that the reward measures the worth of the person who receives it. The target is not inequality as a practical arrangement but the flattering story the fortunate tell themselves about it, the conviction that their advantages are the just wages of virtue rather than the compound interest on a thousand strokes of luck. Stripped of that story, success invites gratitude rather than pride, and failure invites compassion rather than contempt. The able and the lucky may keep their rewards; what they may not keep is the comfortable belief that they are merely receiving their due.

passageSets.title: The Boundaries of the Thinkable
passageSets.passage:
We tend to picture propaganda as a crude business: a dictator's portrait on every wall, a single newspaper printing transparent lies, a population that believes because it is given nothing else to believe. This picture is comforting to citizens of open societies, for it locates propaganda safely elsewhere, in regimes unlike our own, and implies that wherever debate is lively and lies are quickly corrected, manufactured consent cannot exist. The comfort is misplaced. The most sophisticated shaping of opinion does not work by suppressing rival views or by telling falsehoods that fact-checkers might catch. It works, rather, by quietly setting the boundaries of the thinkable, by determining in advance which questions are serious and which are dismissed as naive, which positions count as responsible and which as extreme, so that a furious and apparently free debate can rage entirely within limits that none of the participants chose and few of them notice.

How are such boundaries drawn without anyone drawing them? Largely through the routine, unglamorous habits of respectable journalism itself. Reporters must appear balanced, so they stage debate between two officially sanctioned sides, and silently certify those two sides as the whole of legitimate opinion, banishing anything outside the pair to the wilderness of the unserious. They must rely on credible sources, and the most credible sources are the powerful, whose press offices supply a steady stream of usable material while dissenters offer only inconvenience. They must avoid seeming partisan, so they treat the assumptions shared by both major parties as simple common sense rather than as the contestable premises they often are. Through a thousand such professional reflexes, each defensible on its own, the range of acceptable opinion is mapped out and patrolled, and the citizen who stays within it mistakes the boundary of the debate for the boundary of reality.

This method is not merely subtler than censorship; it is markedly more effective. Overt suppression advertises its own existence. The banned book becomes glamorous, the silenced dissident a martyr, and the citizen who knows he is being managed grows suspicious of his managers. Boundary-setting suffers from none of these weaknesses. It produces no martyrs, because no one is punished; the disfavored view is not forbidden but merely made to seem foolish, and ridicule leaves no scars for sympathy to gather around. Above all, it enlists the citizen's own sense of independence in its service. Having argued passionately within the permitted range, he feels he has thought for himself, and a person who believes he has reached his own conclusions defends them far more stubbornly than one who knows he was told what to think. The chains that bind most securely are the ones the prisoner cannot feel.

To notice all this is not to embrace paranoia, and the argument should not be mistaken for the claim that every newscast is a plot or that nothing reported is true. The facts within the boundary may be perfectly accurate; that is precisely what makes the boundary so hard to see. The remedy, such as it is, lies not in distrusting every fact but in cultivating a stubborn curiosity about the frame: in asking which questions are never asked, whose voices are absent from the respectable debate, and what both sanctioned sides quietly agree to take for granted. A citizen cannot escape all frames, for thought requires some frame or other. But she can refuse to mistake the one she has been handed for the natural shape of the world, and that refusal is the beginning of the only freedom of mind worth the name.

questions.stem: It can be inferred that the author considers boundary-setting more effective than censorship primarily because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: it allows the government to imprison dissidents more efficiently

choices.label: B
choices.text: it requires telling more convincing lies than censorship does

choices.label: C
choices.text: it leaves citizens feeling free and self-directed, so they defend the resulting views as their own

choices.label: D
choices.text: it is cheaper to operate than a system of formal censors

questions.explanation: C is correct because the author argues boundary-setting enlists the citizen's sense of independence, producing beliefs defended more stubbornly. A is wrong because boundary-setting produces no prisoners, which is part of why it works. B is wrong because the author says it need not rely on catchable falsehoods at all. D is wrong because cost is never the author's stated reason; the reason is psychological ownership.

questions.stem: Following the author's advice, a citizen seeking to resist manufactured consent would be best served by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: asking which questions the respectable debate never raises and whose perspectives are missing from it

choices.label: B
choices.text: refusing to believe any fact reported by a major news outlet

choices.label: C
choices.text: choosing whichever of the two mainstream positions seems more reasonable

choices.label: D
choices.text: withdrawing from public affairs entirely to avoid being influenced

questions.explanation: A is correct because the author's remedy is curiosity about the frame, including unasked questions and absent voices. B is wrong because the author warns against distrusting every fact, since facts within the boundary may be true. C is wrong because choosing between the two sanctioned sides stays inside the very boundary at issue. D is wrong because the author seeks freedom of mind, not withdrawal, and never recommends disengagement.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most strengthen the author's claim that consent can be manufactured without censorship or false statements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An analysis finds that on a major issue, coverage across rival outlets was factually accurate yet uniformly excluded an entire range of policy options from discussion

choices.label: B
choices.text: A study shows that a government banned several books and jailed their authors

choices.label: C
choices.text: A survey finds that audiences distrust news outlets they consider biased

choices.label: D
choices.text: Researchers document numerous outright falsehoods that fact-checkers later corrected

questions.explanation: A is correct because accurate coverage that nonetheless silently omits whole options is exactly the boundary-setting the author describes, with no lies or censorship. B is wrong because book-banning and jailing are overt censorship, the mechanism the author contrasts with boundary-setting. C is wrong because audience distrust of biased outlets does not show consent being manufactured invisibly. D is wrong because documented falsehoods illustrate catchable lies, which the author says are not how sophisticated shaping works.

questions.stem: When the author writes that 'the chains that bind most securely are the ones the prisoner cannot feel,' the statement most nearly means that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: physical imprisonment is the most effective form of social control

choices.label: B
choices.text: people who are literally chained rarely try to escape

choices.label: C
choices.text: constraints on thought are most powerful when those subject to them remain unaware of their existence

choices.label: D
choices.text: freedom is an illusion that no one can ever escape

questions.explanation: C is correct because the metaphor restates the thesis that unnoticed boundaries on opinion control more firmly than visible suppression. A is wrong because the passage concerns control of opinion, not physical imprisonment. B is wrong because it is not a literal observation about prisoners. D is wrong because the author affirms that a real, if modest, freedom of mind is possible, so freedom is not declared a total illusion.
